problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem C (data): build a model using ONLY past daily price data to produce a daily trade strategy across cash, gold, and bitcoin that maximizes a $1000 portfolio over 9/11/2016–9/11/2021, with 1% gold / 2% bitcoin transaction fees. Gold trades only Mon–Fri; bitcoin trades daily. This Outstanding paper (Team 2229059) builds a deterministic rule/logic-based ("instantaneous logic") trading engine driven by momentum + mean-reversion signals plus an extreme-market override, and reports a final value of ~$220,486 (ROI ~21,948%, ~194% annualized).
models_used: Momentum / technical-analysis signal: price gradient G_t(n) = P_t - P_{t-n+1} combined with a weighted moving average A_t(n); buy requires BOTH gradient>1 AND (P_t - A_t)>1

Reversion-to-mean signal expressed as an integral/area argument around the moving average mu; buy when price falls far below moving average; trade size proportional to the SQUARE of distance from the mean

Profitability metric attached to each signal (momentum profitability proportional to gradient; mean-reversion profitability proportional to squared deviation) used to size trades and pick the better asset
Conservative sell rule S = (1 - f_a)*P - average(fee-adjusted cost basis) - L; sell only when expected profit clears a margin L

Extreme-market-condition override E = G_t(n) - C*mean(historical gradient); triggers when current gradient exceeds ~5x average gradient, then blocks selling until price falls a percentage (delta P) below interval max
No-buy lockout condition activated after an extreme event + recent sell (prevents buying into dead-cat bounces near a peak)
Holding-period constraint of z days after a buy (suppresses fee-churning on small oscillations)
Noise-normalization weight factor W = C * (1/t^2) to scale down signal magnitude over time and equalize gold vs bitcoin signal scales

Grid-search parameter optimization over [T (hold days), N/L (re-entry price as % of max), E (extreme-sell threshold as % of max)] -> optimal set [12, 0.6, 0.89]; plus fee-sensitivity sweep over [gold fee, bitcoin fee]
key_techniques: Causal/online evaluation: at each day the model only uses data seen up to that day — directly honoring the problem's 'only past prices to date' constraint instead of fitting on the full series
Hybrid signal combination: momentum (captures breakouts above MA) + mean reversion (captures dips below MA) cover complementary regimes; combined via a weighted decision
Regime switching: a normal-market logic layer plus an extreme-market override layer that takes precedence, giving the system a finite state machine feel (normal -> extreme -> no-buy lockout)
Signal normalization across heterogeneous assets: the 1/t^2 weight factor cancels the huge scale mismatch between bitcoin and gold so the comparison of trade attractiveness is apples-to-apples
Explicit fee-aware decision rules: fees baked into both the sell margin S and buy decisions, with a holding period to avoid fee-bleeding high-frequency churn
Trade SIZING (not just direction): profitability metric proportional to gradient / squared deviation lets the model scale position size to signal strength
Parameter tuning by exhaustive grid search with a clearly tabulated Poor/Good/Great/Best ranking of 27 parameter combinations

Validation by zooming into specific buy/sell event windows (bitcoin t=1610-1760, gold) and narrating that orders fire near local troughs/peaks and that the no-buy lockout correctly suppresses bad re-entries
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on FIT-TO-PROBLEM and TRANSPARENCY rather than mathematical sophistication. (1) It rigorously respects the 'use only past data' rule via a causal day-by-day simulation — many teams accidentally peek at future data, and avoiding that is a credibility differentiator. (2) Every component is motivated by a concrete failure mode the team anticipated (fee churn -> holding period; premature sell in a rally -> extreme override; dead-cat bounce -> no-buy lockout; scale mismatch -> 1/t^2 weight). This 'design driven by named pitfalls' reads as real trader/modeler insight. (3) It demonstrates sensitivity analysis on BOTH parameters (27-row table) and transaction fees (the exact robustness checks the prompt asks for), showing the result isn't a fluke. (4) Strong, well-structured communication: clean assumptions-with-justifications, a flowchart of the buy logic, a results narrative tied back to the price charts, and a polished investor MEMORANDUM that satisfies the deliverable. (5) A headline, falsifiable result ($1000 -> ~$220k, 194% annualized) that is easy to grade and clearly 'beats the market.'
section_highlights: Structure is textbook MCM and judge-friendly: Restatement -> General Definitions (defines momentum, volatility, mean reversion, etc. so terms aren't ambiguous) -> Assumptions, each stated AND justified (non-zero price, no margin/leverage, price oscillation) -> Problem Analysis with the bitcoin/gold scale-mismatch plots motivating the normalization -> a 'Calculating and Simplifying the Model' section that introduces each rule (momentum, reversion, profitability, selling, extreme conditions, no-buy, holding, daily order-of-operations, noise normalization) in the exact order they execute, mirroring the algorithm flow -> Results with the equity curve, weight-factor effect, the 27-combo parameter-optimization table, and the fee-sensitivity table -> Validation by zooming into real buy/sell windows and narrating them against the price chart -> Strengths/Weaknesses -> Conclusions -> a complete investor Memorandum (the required deliverable) -> References (mostly Investopedia/Wolfram, acceptable for a trading-strategy framing) -> Appendix with pseudocode class skeleton (Portfolio/Market classes, run() loop). The buy-logic flowchart and the daily order-of-operations subsection are standout clarity devices.
pitfalls_or_limitations: No genuine forecasting / statistical model — it is a hand-tuned heuristic rule engine. There is no ARIMA/GARCH/LSTM/Bayesian forecast, no volatility model, and no probabilistic confidence in predictions; the team even calls it 'a fixed logic system'

High overfitting risk, which the team admits: optimal parameters [12,0.6,0.89] were grid-searched on the SAME single price history they report results on — no train/test split, walk-forward, or out-of-sample/Monte-Carlo validation. The reported ROI could be curve-fit to one realized path

Strategy is essentially a leveraged bet on a single asset (bitcoin) over a historically extraordinary bull run; the ~$220k result is heavily driven by one regime and would likely look very different on a bitcoin bear market or a different 5-year window

Mathematical formalism is loose/inconsistent: the mean-reversion 'integral' inequality is hand-wavy notation, the gradient/MA '>1' thresholds are dimensionally odd (comparing a price difference to the number 1), and the summation index in the sell formula (divide by i vs n) is sloppy
Weak risk treatment: no Sharpe ratio, drawdown, variance, or risk-adjusted return — only raw final dollars; 'beats the market' is asserted without a benchmark comparison (e.g., buy-and-hold bitcoin)

Figures appear to be schematic/illustrative and several charts (e.g., Figure 6) are effectively empty or hard to read; the parameter labels are inconsistent (T N E vs T L E), and the gold validation figure caption references a different time window than the text
Code is only pseudocode skeletons (empty method bodies) in the appendix, so reproducibility is limited
Assumptions (price never hits zero, price oscillates) are convenient rather than rigorously defended, and the 'no margin' choice sidesteps rather than models real day-trading
reusable_patterns: Causal day-by-day simulation loop as the backbone for any 'use only past data' time-series decision problem — iterate over days, expose only history up to t, accumulate equity curve. This is the single most transferable idea for MCM-C-style sequential-decision problems

'Design each rule to defeat a named failure mode' as a writing/modeling discipline: list the concrete ways a naive model fails (fee churn, premature sell, dead-cat bounce, scale mismatch), then introduce ONE mechanism per failure (holding period, extreme override, no-buy lockout, normalization weight). Reads as insight and structures the methodology section

Regime-switching / finite-state-machine architecture: a normal-conditions policy plus a higher-priority override policy for tail events — broadly reusable wherever behavior must change in extreme regimes

Cross-asset signal normalization (e.g., 1/t^2 or any scaling weight) so heterogeneous quantities can be compared on one decision axis — useful any time you rank options measured in different units/scales

Explicit cost/penalty-aware decision rule: bake the transaction fee (or any action cost) directly into the buy/sell inequality plus a profit margin L and a holding/cooldown period to prevent over-trading — generalizes to any problem with per-action costs

Two-tier sensitivity analysis pattern: a parameter grid-search table ranked Poor/Good/Great/Best AND a separate sweep over the externally-specified cost (fees) — exactly the robustness evidence MCM-C judges reward; reuse the ranked-table presentation

Validation-by-zoom: instead of only reporting an aggregate metric, zoom into a representative event window and narrate that the model's actions fire at the right places relative to the raw signal — a persuasive, cheap validation technique

Deliverable-as-document: ending with the exact required artifact (here an investor memorandum) written in-voice, plus an assumptions-with-justification table and a logic flowchart — strong, repeatable communication scaffolding
